title: Pinch Pot Mini Succulent Planter
subtitle: A small hand-pinched pot for a desk or windowsill succulent
excerpt: Form a small pinch pot 8-10 cm in diameter from air-dry clay, add a drainage hole, seal the interior with neat PVA, and you have a lightweight planter for a single small succulent. No kiln or wheel needed.
sourceNotes: Technique derived from standard pinch-pot hand-building methods adapted for air-dry clay.

glossaryTerms.definition: A pot formed by pressing the thumb into a ball of clay and pinching the walls outward and upward to create a hollow vessel
The stage at which clay holds its shape and no longer sticks to your hands, but still contains moisture and can be carved or smoothed
The measurement of how thick the sides of a clay vessel are; even wall thickness helps a piece dry without cracking

content.text: A
pinch pot

is the most direct form in ceramics: a ball of clay, two thumbs, and a little patience. This version makes a small planter 8-10 cm wide, suitable for a single succulent on a desk or windowsill. Sealing the interior with neat PVA makes it water-resistant enough for a plant with infrequent watering. It is not suitable for food, drink, or plants that need daily watering. No kiln or wheel required.

materials.name: PVA glue (neat, undiluted for interior seal)
Grit or small stones for base drainage layer

content.text: Preparation
Knead the clay for 2 minutes until soft and smooth. Work out any air pockets by pressing and folding the clay repeatedly.
Roll the clay into a smooth ball roughly the size of a large orange. Place it on a sheet of baking paper.
Forming the pot
Press your thumb into the centre of the ball to about half the ball's depth. Keep your thumb pointed straight down. This is the first
pinch pot
opening.
Pinch the walls between your thumb (inside) and index finger (outside), rotating the ball a little between each pinch. Aim for even
wall thickness
of around 6-7 mm throughout.
Work from the base upward. Pinch a full circuit of the pot at the base first, then move your thumb higher for the next row, gradually widening the opening to 8-10 cm.
Keep the rim even by pinching it gently between two fingers. If the rim cracks, dab water onto the crack and smooth it closed with a damp fingertip.
Smooth the exterior with the damp pottery sponge, pressing lightly in downward strokes. This closes small surface cracks and gives a cleaner finish.
Adding a drainage hole
While the clay is still soft, turn the pot upside down. Use the needle tool to pierce one hole in the centre of the base. Make it 6-8 mm in diameter by rotating the needle tool in small circles.
Smooth the clay around the hole on the inside. The drainage hole prevents water pooling around the roots.
Drying and sealing
Place the pot right-side up on baking paper. Cover loosely with a plastic bag for the first 6 hours to slow drying. At
leather-hard
stage, remove the bag and leave to dry fully. Allow 2-3 days for a 6-7 mm wall thickness.
Once completely dry, paint the exterior with acrylics if desired.

Apply two coats of neat PVA (undiluted) to the interior only using the mop brush, covering every surface inside including the base. Allow each coat to dry for 1 hour. Neat PVA gives better water resistance than diluted. Do not apply PVA to the drainage hole.
Apply two coats of diluted PVA (1:1 with water) to the exterior for a lightly sealed finish. Leave 24 hours before planting.
Place a small piece of fine mesh or a pebble over the drainage hole before adding grit and compost to prevent blocking.
Variations
Press texture into the exterior while still soft using a stamp, fork, or lace fabric.
Make a set of three in slightly different sizes for a grouped display on a windowsill.
Widen the opening to 12 cm for a larger pot suitable for a small cacti arrangement.
Roll a coil and attach it as a rim detail while the pot is still soft, scoring and slipping the join.

items.problem: The walls are uneven -- thick at the base and thin near the rim
items.cause: Pinching started too high rather than at the base
items.fix: Begin every pinch circuit at the very base of the pot and work upward. Check thickness regularly by pressing a fingertip gently against the wall.

items.cause: Clay dried out too quickly during pinching
items.fix: Dab the rim with water from the sponge every few minutes during forming. Work in a cool room away from direct sunlight.

items.problem: The base is very flat rather than slightly rounded
items.cause: The pot was rested on a hard flat surface while soft
items.fix: Once the basic shape is formed, cradle the pot in your hands and add a gentle curve to the base with your thumbs before setting it down.

items.problem: Water seeped through the pot wall despite PVA sealing
items.cause: Only one coat of PVA was applied or the PVA was too diluted
items.fix: Apply two coats of neat PVA inside and allow full drying between coats. Place the pot inside a matching saucer to catch any seepage.
